body_txt: It starts with the hero about to go into a big fight, maybe against the Big Bad or at least one of his most powerful minions. He's gonna need all the help he can get, but one of his allies — likely the one he's had the least amount of trust in anyway through this whole ordeal—just wants to leave when he has the chance . The guy will call it self-preservation or common sense and remind the hero that he's Not in This for Your Revolution . The hero may chide him for being selfish, but he can live with that. "Fine, be that way!" the hero shouts, and they part company, likely on a sour note. Now, with the hero left alone, the fight looks lost. It's clearly his Darkest Hour , and if he remembers what his friend said at all, he may be cursing him, or even worse, start wondering whether the guy had a point. But no! At the last moment that same untrustworthy ally in question returns to save the protagonist. Why? Changed his mind, for whatever reason. A way of setting up Big Damn Heroes . Friends can let the hero down for many reasons. Coming back usually entails character growth. Compare Conscience Makes You Go Back , Achilles in His Tent , 10-Minute Retirement . For the romance/gender-related version after Unsettling Gender-Reveal , see If It's You, It's Okay .
